title: The Road Racing Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to train for and race gran fondos, sportives, and centuries.
meta_description: A complete road racing training guide — from building your aerobic engine to pack tactics, fueling, and race-week execution. Written by coaches who race.

chapters.title: What Is Road Racing?
chapters.subtitle: Fondos, sportives, and the events you're actually training for

blocks.content: Road racing, for most of us, means the mass-participation events that have exploded over the last two decades: gran fondos, sportives, centuries, the big timed climbs. You're not racing a 90-rider pro peloton. You're chasing a number on a finish-line clock, a personal best, or the wheel of the rider who dropped you last year.

What makes the road different from gravel or the trail is the pack. Drafting is the whole game — sit in the right wheel and you save 25-30% of your energy; get caught in the wind alone and the day falls apart. The surface is predictable; the tactics are not. Positioning, timing, and knowing when to spend your matches matter more than raw watts.

This guide is built around that reality: an aerobic engine big enough to finish strong, the threshold to hold a hard group, and the handling to stay safe and efficient in a bunch. Everything here is written by coaches who actually pin numbers on.

chapters.title: Choosing Your Race
chapters.subtitle: Pick the event that fits your engine and your calendar

blocks.content: The race you pick determines the plan you need. A flat 100-mile fondo rewards sustained tempo and pack craft; a mountainous sportive with 10,000 ft of climbing rewards a high power-to-weight and the discipline to ride your own pace up the cols. Pick the demands first, then build toward them.

Give yourself runway. A first century or fondo wants 14-18 weeks from a real base; if you're starting from the couch, more. Slot one A-event per season and build everything around it — B-events become hard training days, not goals that fragment your focus.
Be honest about the climbing. The single biggest predictor of how your day goes on a hilly course is your weight-adjusted threshold. If the route climbs, the plan climbs.

chapters.title: Building the Engine
chapters.subtitle: Base, then build — the aerobic foundation under everything

blocks.content: Endurance is the foundation, and there's no shortcut around it. The long ride is the single most important session in your week: it builds the fat-burning, capillary-dense aerobic engine that lets you hold power deep into a long event. Most of your hours should be genuinely easy — conversational, controlled, boring on purpose.

Boredom is the skill nobody advertises. The riders who improve are the ones who can sit in zone 2 for three hours without turning it into a smash-fest. Volume you can repeat beats heroic days you need a week to recover from.

Build progressively. Add no more than ~10% volume per week, take a recovery week every third or fourth, and let the long ride grow toward (but not absurdly past) your event duration. The engine is built in months, not weekends.

chapters.title: Workout Execution
chapters.subtitle: Zones, intervals, and hitting the targets that matter

blocks.content: Once the base is in, intensity sharpens it. Train by zones, not feelings: zone 2 builds the engine, threshold (around your one-hour power) raises the ceiling you can sustain, and VO2max work — short, brutal 3-5 minute efforts — lifts the top end. The trap is the gray zone: zone 3 is too hard to recover from and too easy to drive real adaptation. Ride it only when prescribed.

Power is the primary target; heart rate is the cross-check. If you're HR-based, your zones are a percentage of threshold HR — not max HR. On a hot or fatigued day, trust HR over the number on the screen.

Two to three quality days a week is plenty for most road athletes. Keep hard days hard and easy days genuinely easy. The plan controls how much time you spend in each zone; your only job is to hit the prescribed zone when the workout calls for it.

chapters.title: Fueling for the Distance
chapters.subtitle: Carbs per hour, hydration, and a gut that can take it

blocks.content: Road events are won and lost at the food table as much as on the climbs. For anything over ~90 minutes you need carbohydrate — 60-90+ grams per hour for a hard, long day, far more than most riders eat. Bonking isn't a willpower failure; it's an empty tank you could have kept full.

Your gut is trainable. Practice race-day fueling on your long rides — the exact gels, drink mix, and real food you'll use — so race day is a rehearsal, not an experiment. A stomach that's never seen 80g/hr will reject it when you need it most.
Hydrate to thirst plus a plan: know your sweat rate, carry enough, and don't forget sodium on hot days. Start fueling early, before you feel empty — by the time you're hungry, you're already behind.

chapters.title: Pack Skills & Race Tactics
chapters.subtitle: Drafting, echelons, positioning, and when to spend

blocks.content: The pack is the road racer's superpower and biggest risk. Sit 30-60cm off the wheel ahead — close enough to draft, far enough to react — look through the riders in front, not at the wheel, and match speed with feathered brakes, never grabs. Surging and braking is how gaps and crashes start.

In a crosswind the shelter sits diagonally behind and to the leeward side — that's an echelon, and when the road tilts into the wind, the front splits into groups. Know which way the wind comes from, fight for the front before the exposed section, and never get caught in the gutter.

Spend your matches deliberately. Move up before climbs and corners, not during them; cover the moves that matter and let the rest go. The strongest rider doesn't always win — the one who wastes the least energy getting to the finish does.

chapters.title: Race Week
chapters.subtitle: Taper, prep, and arriving sharp instead of stale

blocks.content: The work is done; race week is about arriving fresh, not fit. Cut volume by 40-60% while keeping a few short, sharp openers so the legs stay snappy. You can't add fitness in the final week — you can only blunt it by training through fatigue, or sharpen it by resting smart.

Dial the logistics: know the route profile, the feed zones, the weather, and your pacing plan before you pin a number. Lay out kit and nutrition the night before. The calmer the morning, the better the day.
Trust the taper even when you feel sluggish — that heavy-legged feeling mid-week is normal as fatigue clears. Race-morning openers and adrenaline bring the legs back.

chapters.title: After the Finish
chapters.subtitle: Recovery, reflection, and building the next season

blocks.content: The finish line is a checkpoint, not the end. The first 48 hours are about real recovery: eat, sleep, easy spinning only. The fitness you built doesn't vanish — protect it with rest now and you carry it into the next block instead of digging a hole.

Review honestly. Where did the day come apart — fueling, pacing, positioning, the legs? The race is the most unfakeable feedback you'll get all year; mine it for the one or two things to fix, not a list of twenty.

Then build forward. A short, deliberate off-season, a fresh base, and a new A-event give the next plan a target. Compounding small, repeatable work is how riders get genuinely fast — season over season, not week over week.
